scenario: Draft a plaint for recovery of Rs.15,00,000 given as a hand loan. The plaintiff Ram Kumar, aged 45, resident of Jayanagar, Bengaluru, advanced Rs.15,00,000 to the defendant Suresh Patel, aged 38, resident of Koramangala, Bengaluru, on 15.03.2024 via NEFT bank transfer (UTR: AXIB20240315123456). There was no written agreement. The defendant acknowledged the loan via WhatsApp messages. Despite repeated oral demands and a legal notice dated 01.07.2024, the defendant has failed to repay. The plaintiff claims interest at 12% per annum from the date of default (20.06.2024). File in City Civil Court, Bengaluru.

scorecard.detail: At least one statutory section cited

draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE CITY CIVIL COURT AT BENGALURU
O.S. No. __________ / 20____

RAM KUMAR,
Aged about 45 years,
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: Jayanagar, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

SURESHP PATEL,
Aged about 38 years,
Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: Koramangala, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. 15,00,000/- WITH INTEREST AND COSTS

FROM THE DEFENDANT ON LOAN/BORROWED MONEY

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff Ram Kumar is a person of sound mind and is competent to file the present suit. The Defendant Suresh Patel is a person capable of being sued. This Hon'ble Court has territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction to entertain and try the suit, as the loan was advanced in Bengaluru and the Defendant resides at Koramangala, Bengaluru, which falls within the local limits of this Court, and the value of the suit is within the pecuniary jurisdiction of this Court.

FACTS OF THE CASE

2. The Plaintiff Ram Kumar, aged 45 years, residing at Jayanagar, Bengaluru, advanced a loan of Rs. 15,00,000 (Rupees Fifteen Lakh Only) to the Defendant Suresh Patel on 15.03.2024 via NEFT bank transfer, the transaction being evidenced by the Bank Transfer Record with UTR AXIB20240315123456 (Annexure A). There was no written loan agreement between the parties.

3. The Defendant acknowledged the loan through WhatsApp messages, the contents of which are annexed herewith as Annexure B and form part of the evidence of the loan and its terms. The WhatsApp messages clearly indicate acknowledgment of the loan by the Defendant.

4. The Plaintiff, in good faith, delivered the loan amount of Rs. 15,00,000 to the Defendant on 15.03.2024 through the aforementioned NEFT transfer (Annexure A). There was no written repayment schedule or written agreement, and the loan was thus a hand loan evidenced by verbal terms and the WhatsApp acknowledgment.

5. The Defendant defaulted in repayment on 20.06.2024 by failing to repay the principal amount and any interest that may have accrued. The Plaintiff has, on several occasions, orally demanded repayment of the loan in full, but the Defendant has remained silent or evasive regarding payment.

6. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 01.07.2024 to the Defendant demanding repayment of the principal sum of Rs. 15,00,000 along with lawful interest from 20.06.2024 until realization and costs of the notice and suit (a true copy of the legal notice is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure C). The Defendant did not comply with the demands contained in the legal notice.

7. The principal loan amount remains unpaid. The Plaintiff is entitled to recover the principal sum of Rs. 15,00,000 and interest at the rate of 12% per annum from the date of default (20.06.2024) until actual realization. The Plaintiff claims this interest as a consequence of the Defendant’s breach of the loan arrangement evidenced by the WhatsApp acknowledgment and NEFT transfer.

8. The Defendant’s refusal to repay despite demand constitutes a clear breach of obligation arising from the loan transaction, evidenced by Annexures A and B, and reinforced by Annexure C (the legal notice). The Plaintiff has no other adequate remedy except a suit for recovery of the said amount and costs.

LEGAL BASIS

9. The Plaintiff relies on the Indian Contract Act, 1872, particularly Section 65 and Section 70, as the legal basis for recovery of money lent and payable on demand, and for enforcing obligations arising from the loan transaction evidenced by the WhatsApp acknowledgment and NEFT transfer (Annexures A and B). The loan was an enforceable contract, and the Defendant’s failure to repay constitutes a breach of contract.

10. In the alternative, without prejudice to the above, the Plaintiff submits that the Defendant is liable in damages for breach of contract under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, in the event the Court finds that any portion of the consideration purportedly provided by the Plaintiff failed in law or that the facts fall within the scope of Section 73.

CAUSE OF ACTION

11. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on 20.06.2024, when the Defendant failed and refused to repay the loan amount of Rs. 15,00,000 despite the Plaintiff’s performance in lending the same on 15.03.2024 (Annexure A) and the Defendant’s acknowledgment of the loan via WhatsApp messages (Annexure B).

12. The cause of action further arose on 01.07.2024 when the Plaintiff served the legal notice demanding repayment (Annexure C). The Defendant’s failure to comply with the demand confirms the continuing breach and gives the Plaintiff the right to sue for recovery.

13. The cause of action is a continuing one, as the Defendant continues to wrongfully retain the Plaintiff’s money, thus sustaining the Plaintiff’s claim for recovery of Rs. 1
